title: Campaign Manager
setting: Campaign HQ (usually a rented shopfront) + Field Operations Across the Electorate
companySize: Campaign team of 3-5 paid staff + 50-500 volunteers depending on the campaign
description: The campaign manager is responsible for everything: strategy, budget, volunteers, media, events, fundraising, and getting the candidate elected. It is one of the most intense jobs in politics — a fixed deadline (election day), limited resources, and total accountability for the result. Campaign managers live and breathe the campaign for 3-12 months, then it is over. Win or lose, the day after election day, the job ends.
salary: $80,000-$150,000 AUD for a federal campaign (often contract/fixed-term) / volunteer for local campaigns

schedule.activity: Morning media scan and social media check — is there anything overnight that changes today's plan?
schedule.note: The campaign manager wakes up thinking about what can go wrong today.

schedule.activity: Call with the candidate — brief on today's schedule, key messages, any emerging issues to address or avoid
Staff huddle at campaign HQ — assign tasks for the day: media, field, digital, events, fundraising
Review field data from yesterday's door-knocking — how many doors, how many contacts, persuasion rate, any issues raised
schedule.note: The numbers from the field are the campaign's vital signs. If contact rates are dropping, something is wrong.

schedule.activity: Coordinate a media opportunity — brief the candidate, set up the location, manage journalist logistics
Fundraising check-in — review donation targets, plan the next fundraising email or event, update the budget
Lunch with a key volunteer leader — keep morale up, hear concerns, reinforce how their work matters
schedule.note: Volunteer management is people management. The best campaigns run on relationships, not orders.

schedule.activity: Strategy session — review polling data, adjust targeting, decide whether to shift resources to a different area
Problem-solving — a volunteer quit, a corflute was vandalised, the opposition launched an attack ad. Handle it all
schedule.note: A campaign manager's most important skill is triage — deciding which fires to fight and which to let burn.

schedule.activity: Oversee evening door-knocking launch — brief volunteers, distribute walk lists, send teams out
Attend a community event with the candidate — work the room, make introductions, gather intelligence
schedule.note: The campaign manager is always on. Every interaction is data.

schedule.activity: Review the day, update the campaign tracker, plan tomorrow, send the overnight brief to the candidate
Go home. Try to sleep. Think about what you forgot
schedule.note: Campaign managers rarely take days off during the final 8 weeks. It is an endurance event.

responsibilities: Develop and execute the overall campaign strategy
Manage the campaign budget — every dollar must be accounted for
Recruit, train, and retain volunteers — the campaign's most valuable resource
Coordinate field operations: door-knocking, phone banks, polling booth coverage
Manage earned and paid media strategy
Oversee fundraising targets and donor relations
Keep the candidate on-message, on-schedule, and mentally prepared
Run election day operations: scrutineers, how-to-vote distribution, turnout
challenges: Total accountability with limited control — you manage the plan but the candidate faces the voters
Resource scarcity — never enough money, volunteers, or time
Managing a candidate who may not follow your advice
Opponent attacks that require rapid, disciplined responses
The emotional weight of a binary outcome — win or lose, with no middle ground
rewards: The most intense professional challenge most people will ever face
The satisfaction of building something from nothing — a team, a movement, a result
Deep relationships forged under pressure with staff and volunteers
If you win: the knowledge that your work changed the outcome
Transferable skills in strategy, leadership, and crisis management that apply to any career
careerPath: Campaign manager for a local or state campaign
Campaign manager or director for a federal campaign
Transition to: party director, political consultancy, corporate strategy, or running for office yourself
